Executive Summary: Risk Factors Associated with Stroke in an Adult Population

BioCore Clinical Data Analytics Team

July 01, 2026

Target Audience: Hospital Leadership, Medical Director, and Quality Committee
Cohort Size: 4,253 Adult Patients (Retrospective Observational Analysis)

1. Key Findings

Following a rigorous data quality assessment and multivariate statistical analysis of 4,253 adult patients, the following primary insights were extracted regarding stroke risk:

  • Age is the Dominant Predictor: Advancing age is the single most powerful driver of stroke. The risk increases exponentially, with the condition being virtually non-existent in patients under 40 and spiking significantly in those over 60.
  • Metabolic & Vascular Biomarkers Drive Modifiable Risk: After controlling for age and gender, Hypertension and Elevated Fasting Glucose (>125 mg/dL) emerged as the most significant independent, modifiable predictors of stroke.
  • The ‘Marriage’ Confounder: Initial unadjusted data suggested married individuals had a drastically higher stroke risk. However, multivariate analysis proved this is a statistical illusion (confounding bias); married patients in this cohort are simply older. Age, not marital status, is the true biological driver.
  • The BMI Paradox: While extreme Body Mass Index (BMI) visually clusters with stroke cases, it loses primary predictive significance when adjusted for Glucose and Hypertension. This indicates obesity causes stroke indirectly by driving metabolic syndrome, rather than acting as an independent mechanical trigger.


Figure 1. Variable Importance Ranking (Wald Z-Statistic)

2. High-Risk Populations

Based on subgroup analyses and multivariate modeling, hospital resources should recognize the following cohorts as exceptionally high-risk:

  1. The Geriatric Cohort (Age > 60): This demographic holds the highest absolute baseline prevalence of stroke.
  2. The Diabetic/Pre-Diabetic Phenotype: Patients presenting with fasting glucose levels exceeding 125 mg/dL.
  3. The Cardiovascular Comorbidity Cohort: Patients with established histories of hypertension or baseline heart disease, regardless of their current age or gender.


Figure 2. Stroke Prevalence Disparities Across Clinical Subgroups


Figure 3. Multivariate Odds Ratios (Forest Plot)

3. Clinical Implications

The data clearly demonstrates that while we cannot change a patient’s age (the primary risk factor), the secondary drivers of stroke are highly responsive to medical intervention. The predictive model achieved an AUC of >0.80, proving that standard triage vitals (Age, BP, Glucose) are highly effective at discriminating stroke risk without the immediate need for expensive, advanced neuroimaging during initial risk stratification.

Furthermore, the data implies that subjective lifestyle questionnaires (e.g., employment type, marital status) are mathematically inferior to objective clinical biomarkers when predicting acute cerebrovascular events.


Figure 4. Diagnostic Performance (ROC Curve)

4. Recommendations for Screening

To optimize resource allocation and improve early detection, we recommend the following structural changes to screening protocols:

  • Age-Stratified Triage Pathways: Implement an automated stroke-risk flag in the Electronic Health Record (EHR) for all patients over the age of 60 presenting to primary care or emergency triage.
  • Mandatory Metabolic Panels: Require fasting glucose and rigorous blood pressure evaluations as a mandatory screening baseline for any patient over 40 reporting transient neurological symptoms, prioritizing these objective metrics over lifestyle surveys.
  • Optimized Screening Thresholds: Because stroke is a rare but fatal event (~5% cohort prevalence), screening algorithms should be tuned for Sensitivity rather than pure Precision. We must tolerate a higher rate of false-positive screenings (leading to preventative consultations) to ensure we do not miss true-positive stroke risks.

5. Recommendations for Preventive Programs

  • Aggressive Glycemic & BP Control: Launch targeted outpatient intervention programs focused strictly on glycemic control and anti-hypertensive medication adherence for the 40–60 age group to delay the exponential risk spike seen after age 60.
  • Metabolic Syndrome Clinics: Shift preventive focus away from pure weight-loss (BMI) campaigns and pivot toward comprehensive metabolic clinics that treat the triad of obesity, hypertension, and hyperglycemia simultaneously.


Figure 5. Continuous Risk Factor Distributions

6. Limitations of the Analysis

Hospital leadership must interpret these findings within the context of the study’s limitations:

  • Retrospective Observational Design: This data establishes strong mathematical associations but cannot definitively prove causality.
  • Missing Data Imputation: Approximately 4% of the cohort was missing BMI data, likely due to emergency triage omissions. While we rigorously imputed this using cohort medians, it may obscure nuanced risks at the extreme ends of morbid obesity.
  • Class Imbalance: Stroke is a rare event. While our statistical methods (Youden’s J Statistic, Logistic Regression) account for this, predictive models trained on imbalanced data naturally suffer from low Positive Predictive Value (Precision).

7. Suggested Future Work

To transition this analytical pipeline into a localized clinical asset, we recommend the following next steps:

  • Prospective Validation: Deploy the logistic regression scoring algorithm in a shadow-mode prospective trial within the hospital EHR to validate its real-world accuracy on incoming patient cohorts.
  • Longitudinal Integration: Update the dataset to include longitudinal vitals (e.g., HbA1c over 5 years rather than a single fasting glucose snapshot) to measure how the duration of metabolic disease impacts stroke risk.
  • Machine Learning Deployment: Transition this baseline logistic model into an advanced machine learning framework (e.g., Random Forest or XGBoost) capable of running silently in the EHR background, sending automated, real-time preventive alerts to primary care physicians during routine check-ups.
